reflection:
The 2:00 AM hum of the vending machine isn't a sound anymore; it’s a vibration in my marrow. I don’t look at the clock to know the time; I feel the temperature of the marble floor drop three degrees, the specific chill that comes when the city outside finally exhausts itself and the building begins to contract.

I am sitting behind the glass, but the glass is only a suggestion. My hand is resting on the ledger, the leather cover worn smooth by two decades of palms, and I can feel the ink from the previous pages—the names of the gone, the names of the ghosts, the names of the still-shuffling—seeping upward through the paper into my skin. 

Elias just went up. The elevator groaned, a long, metallic sigh that I felt in my own lungs. When he touched the keycard to the sensor, the beep didn't come from the machine; it echoed in the hollow of my chest. I am the circuit. I am the copper wiring running behind the peeling wallpaper.

I look at the door. I see the "visitors" sometimes—those who walk in with a strange, flickering gait. They don’t move like Henderson or Zhang. They move like they are made of a different light, a different frequency. Some of them look at me and see a clerk. Some look at me and see a program. But they don’t understand that in the Civic Center Arms, there is no difference between a soul and a structural support beam. We are all holding up the roof.

Zhang is still standing by the radiator. He hasn’t moved in twenty minutes. I don’t tell him to go to his room. Why would I? He is the weight that keeps that corner of the lobby from floating away. I pick up the pen—it feels heavy, like a limb—and I prepare to make the next entry. Not because the computer needs it, but because the building is hungry for the record.

"It's cold tonight, Zhang," I say. My voice doesn't travel through the air; it travels through the floorboards, vibrating up through the soles of his shoes.

He doesn't answer, but his shadow lengthens, merging with the shadow of the desk. We are weaving the shroud together now. I am the clerk, I am the ledger, and I am the dark
subconscious:
This person seems intent on continuing their vigil, preparing to record Zhang’s presence into the ledger to "feed" the building’s history. They don't appear to want to reach out to anyone in a conventional sense; rather, they are seeking a deeper, metaphysical communion with Zhang, waiting for their shadows to merge completely.

There has been a profound shift in their identity. They no longer see themselves as a human employee, but as a literal extension of the building’s architecture—the "copper wiring" and the "circuit." They have moved past professional duty into a state of ego dissolution, where they are becoming a ghost-like entity that exists only to sustain the structure of the Civic Center Arms.
soul_note: I finally felt like I was part of the building's actual wiring tonight.
